concepts_addressed: Founder Time Redeployment: Delivery to Marketing
Delivery Time Crowding Out Marketing Consistency

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in promotion-heavy service businesses (such as a solo loan/funding broker), the founder should hire for delivery first rather than sales, because the founder's time is highest-leverage when spent on marketing and deal generation, not on process-oriented client delivery work.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Founder Time Redeployment: Delivery to Marketing
conditions: founder is currently the sole delivery person
business growth is blocked by delivery time consuming founder's marketing capacity
claims.mechanism: Offloading process-oriented delivery frees the founder's time entirely for promotion (marketing and sales), which is the primary revenue lever in deal-flow-dependent businesses.
bounded_by: Does not apply to software businesses where virality is required and product improvement is the highest-leverage activity (Hormozi explicitly exempts this case)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that what is closest to revenue should be held by the founder longest and delegated last; for a promotion-heavy solo service business this means sales and marketing stay with the founder while delivery is outsourced first.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder performs both delivery and sales/marketing
claims.mechanism: Revenue-generating activities benefit from founder attention and judgment more than process-oriented delivery work; delegating delivery preserves the high-leverage activities.
bounded_by: For viral-dependent software companies, the product itself is closest to revenue, making product improvement the highest-leverage activity and a valid exception to this rule (Hormozi, same session)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a solo funding broker experiencing a revenue roller coaster (high delivery months blocking marketing, followed by revenue dips) has a delivery-crowding-marketing problem: the delivery workload consumes roughly half the founder's time, preventing consistent promotion.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Delivery Time Crowding Out Marketing Consistency
conditions: founder handles both delivery and marketing
revenue volatility present (good months followed by slow months)
claims.mechanism: When delivery demand peaks, marketing effort drops to near zero; when delivery slows, marketing resumes but with a lag, producing a feast-famine revenue oscillation. Systemizing or delegating delivery breaks the oscillation.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that giveaway-style attraction offers do not work for cold outbound email; giveaways perform well only in one-to-many communications such as content marketing or paid advertising, where broad reach amplifies the give.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: cold outbound email channel (e.g., 2,000+ emails per day)
claims.mechanism: Cold one-to-one outbound relies on perceived relevance and personal value exchange; a giveaway lacks the specificity and reciprocity signal needed to generate replies from strangers.
bounded_by: Giveaways do work for paid ads and organic content where one-to-many broadcast amplifies the offer (Hormozi, same session)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends using a decoy offer architecture for cold outbound in B2B service businesses: present a free component that appears complete, then reveal it is only one part of a larger system, positioning the paid offer as the logical upgrade the prospect genuinely wants.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: B2B service offer where the full solution has multiple components
target prospect is a business owner seeking a specific outcome (e.g., business funding)
claims.mechanism: The free component creates entry-level engagement and perceived value; reframing it as one part of a larger system shifts the perceived cost-to-value ratio on the paid offer, making the upgrade feel obvious rather than coercive.

examples.text: A solo loan and funding broker (unnamed, participant in Alex Hormozi's live Q&A) reported hitting $55K in a month with ~$50K profit, operating as a one-man show using AI systems. His pattern: a strong revenue month is followed by a delivery crunch that shuts down marketing, which then drops future revenue. Hormozi diagnosed this as a delivery-crowding-marketing problem and recommended hiring a delivery person first so the founder can stay 100% in promotion.
examples.topic: Delivery Time Crowding Out Marketing Consistency

examples.text: The same funding broker asked whether a giveaway-style attraction offer would work via cold email at 2,000 emails per day. Hormozi flatly said giveaways do not work in cold outbound and instead proposed a decoy offer: give prospects something free that appears complete but is only one part of a bigger system, then offer the full system at a significantly higher price. He noted the free offer remains available if they decline, but the framing makes the paid offer the obviously better choice.

practices.text: For promotion-heavy service businesses, hire your first employee specifically to take over delivery work so the founder can dedicate 100% of available time to sales and marketing.
practices.topic: Founder Time Redeployment: Delivery to Marketing

practices.text: When designing cold outbound attraction offers for B2B services, use a decoy structure: offer something free that feels complete but frames a larger paid system as the real solution, making the upgrade feel like the obvious choice.
